preFix.title: Engrosser's script — American Copperplate
preFix.subtitle: The formal pointed-pen hand used for certificates and legal documents
preFix.excerpt: Engrosser's script is the highly formal American pointed-pen hand used to engross (write out officially) certificates, diplomas, and legal documents. More upright than Spencerian, with a pronounced slope of 52 to 54 degrees and dramatic thick-thin contrast, it demands consistent pressure control on the pointed nib and absolute regularity of slant. The compound curve, an entrance curve flowing into a body oval and out on an exit shade, is its fundamental building block.
preFix.sourceNotes: Historical lineage from Edward Cocker's penmanship copybooks (17th century, including The Pen's Triumph): public domain. Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866, Spencer Brothers copybooks): public domain. The Spencerian tradition is the direct precursor to American pointed-pen formal hands. Michael Sull's codification of Engrosser's Script is copyright; this entry draws on the structural description of the compound-curve building block and the slant system only, not from Sull's text.

content.text: The word 'engross' originally meant to write out a legal or official document in a large, clear hand.

is the American formal hand that took that name from the job it performed. Its lineage runs from the English Roundhand copybooks of the 17th century, through Spencerian, to a specifically American codification of the 19th century. Compared with Spencerian, it is more upright (a steeper body slope of 52 to 54 degrees rather than Spencerian's 52 degrees at the extreme), wider in its thick strokes, and less florid in its capital alphabet. The result is more austere and better suited to official documents than to personal correspondence.

items.name: Hunt 101 nib
items.substitutions: Nikko G for early practice; Gillott 303 for advanced hairline work

items.name: Oblique pen holder
items.substitutions: Straight holder if left-handed (and use a left oblique holder)

items.name: Iron-gall ink (modern formulation)
items.substitutions: Black Sumi ink at slightly higher viscosity also works

items.name: Tracing paper
items.substitutions: For tracing the slant grid under practice sheets

items.name: Ruling pen or HB pencil
items.substitutions: For ruling slant lines before each practice session

content.text: Rule a full grid of guide lines before writing. Baselines and x-height lines at 5 mm apart (adjust to suit your nib size). Rule

at 52 to 54 degrees from the horizontal at 3 mm intervals across the sheet. Every straight stroke in the alphabet runs parallel to these lines. Learning Engrosser's script without a slant grid is slow work; the grid removes one variable and lets you focus on pressure control.

Fit the nib into the oblique pen holder. The standard oblique holder is designed for right-handed writers. The flange angles the nib to approximately 55 degrees from the baseline, so the slit of the nib points along the slant line with the pen held naturally. The wrist rests on the side of the little finger, arm resting on the forearm. The thumb, index finger, and middle finger hold the holder; the ring and little finger ride on the paper as a support.

Clean and test the Hunt 101 nib before loading with ink. New nibs have a factory-applied protective coating that repels water-based inks. Pass the nib briefly through a flame or rub gently with a cotton swab dipped in a small amount of ammonia solution. Dry the nib and load ink from the underside using the brush or an eyedropper. Draw a test stroke: the ink should release immediately on contact with the paper. If it beads, the coating is still present; repeat the cleaning.
in its overturn form before attempting any letter. The overturn begins as a thin entrance hairline rising from below the baseline, curves to a peak above the x-height, then comes down as a thickening

stroke running along the slant line. The pressure increases as the stroke descends; at the widest point the nib tines are approximately 1 to 1.5 mm apart. The exit is a thinning curve running left along the baseline to begin the next stroke. Practise 20 rows of isolated overturns before adding any connecting strokes.

Practise the underturn form. The underturn begins as a shade stroke descending from above the x-height (pressure applied from the moment the nib touches the paper), curves at the baseline into a thin exit hairline rising to the right. The shade and the hairline are produced by the same continuous motion: the pressure releases precisely as the curve begins. This release point (the base of the underturn) is the critical moment. Release too early and the shade is too short; release too late and the hairline is too thick.

Begin the lowercase alphabet with the letters that use a single compound curve: i (underturn with dot), u (underturn plus underturn), n (overturn plus underturn). Write each letter in rows of ten before moving to the next. The stroke order for each is: Engrosser's i, two strokes: (1) underturn shade and exit; (2) dot above the x-height at the slant-line position. Engrosser's n, two strokes: (1) overturn entry and full shade; (2) underturn with exit.

The capital alphabet is built on compound curves extended with swash loops. Engrosser's capitals are more restrained in their flourishes than Spencerian: the swash loops are large but close back on themselves rather than extending into open spirals. Work through capitals A through Z in stroke order, studying each capital's construction before writing. The capital O is two strokes: a left-side shade curve (pressure on the left arc, thin on the top and bottom) then a right-side thin connector. Every other oval capital follows a similar logic.

attrs.intro: Engrosser's script punishes inconsistent slant and uncontrolled pressure. These are the four failures encountered most often at the intermediate stage.

items.fix: Rule slant lines at 52 to 54 degrees every 3 mm across every practice sheet without exception. Align the nib to the slant line at the start of each word, not just at the start of a row.
items.cause: The slant-line grid was not ruled, or the pen is not aligned to the flange correctly.
items.symptom: The slant wanders across the page

items.fix: Slow down to one-quarter normal writing speed. Watch the nib tines as the stroke descends: the tines should open gradually through the middle of the stroke and close smoothly through the underturn curve. Speed is the enemy at this stage.
items.cause: Pressure is applied inconsistently or released at different points in the underturn curve.
items.symptom: The shade strokes are uneven in width

items.fix: Upstrokes in pointed-pen calligraphy must be made with zero pressure: the nib tines closed, the nib gliding on the tip only. Check that the pen is held at the correct angle to the paper surface: too upright and the nib corner catches. Reduce the pen angle slightly.
items.cause: The upstroke hairlines are being made with too much pressure, or the nib angle is slightly wrong so the corner of the nib is digging in.
items.symptom: The nib scratches and catches on the upstroke

items.fix: Check the shade width against the slant-line spacing: Engrosser's shades occupy approximately one-third of the space between two adjacent slant lines. Reduce the capital flourishes to closed loops rather than open spirals.
items.cause: The shades are too narrow and the script is too florid.
items.symptom: Letters look like Spencerian rather than Engrosser's

attrs.heading: Common problems

content.text: Engrosser's script is the most demanding of the pointed-pen hands in terms of consistency. Once the compound curve is reliable, Spencerian is within reach: the two hands share the same pressure mechanics, with Spencerian requiring less shade width and more flowing capital flourishes. A calligrapher who can hold the Engrosser's slant across a full page can hold any pointed-pen script.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
title contains em or en dash — must be zero anywhere in user-facing copy
historical century reference "17th century" in body — move to sourceNotes or introduce with a person (e.g. "the 17th-century herbalist X")
paragraph reads at grade 11.8 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
